1: shadow Contend, O LORD, with those who contend with me; fight against those who fight against me! Christ is the righteous one surrendered to accusers, abandoned to those who contend against him. God's contending on his behalf is the resurrection — the Father fights for the Son by raising him from the dead, vindicating his cause against every accusation (Acts 2:24).
2: shadow Take hold of shield and buckler and rise for my help! The call for God to rise for the innocent sufferer anticipates the resurrection itself — God rises to help Christ by raising Christ. The divine warrior imagery (shield and buckler) is fulfilled when God defeats sin and death through the cross (Colossians 2:15).
3: shadow Draw the spear and javelin against my pursuers! Say to my soul, 'I am your salvation!' The word I am your salvation (yeshuatekha — from the root of the name Joshua/Jesus) is spoken by God to the righteous sufferer. Christ's name means precisely this: you shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins (Matthew 1:21).
4: shadow Let them be put to shame and dishonored who seek after my life! Those who sought Christ's life — the chief priests, the crowd, Herod — are ultimately shamed by the resurrection. The one they sought to destroy is declared Lord and Christ (Acts 2:36); the cross that was meant to shame him becomes his throne.
5: theme Let them be like chaff before the wind, with the angel of the LORD driving them away! The judgment of the wicked who oppose the righteous is a theme reaching to Christ's parousia: the chaff he will burn with unquenchable fire (Matthew 3:12). The one who was driven away by his enemies will himself drive away the chaff at the final harvest.
6: shadow Let their way be dark and slippery, with the angel of the LORD pursuing them! Darkness as the path of those who oppose Christ is narratively fulfilled in Judas: he immediately went out. And it was night (John 13:30). The one who betrays the light enters the darkness; the slippery path of treachery leads to destruction.
7: direct For without cause they hid their net for me; without cause they dug a pit for my life. Jesus applies the without cause motif of Psalms 35:7 and Psalms 69:4 to himself: they hated me without a cause (John 15:25). The causeless enmity of David's accusers becomes the pattern Christ identifies as his own — unmotivated hatred that fulfills Scripture.
8: shadow Let ruin come upon him when he does not know it, and let the net that he hid ensnare him. The principle of the trapper trapped is fulfilled in the crucifixion-resurrection sequence: those who set the net for Christ are caught in their own schemes. Death itself is destroyed by the death it inflicted; the one cast into the pit becomes the cornerstone (Matthew 21:42).
9: shadow Then my soul will rejoice in the LORD, exulting in his salvation. The joy of the vindicated righteous one is the joy Christ endured the cross for: who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross, despising the shame (Hebrews 12:2). The resurrection brings the exulting in salvation that the lament anticipated.
10: shadow All my bones shall say, 'O LORD, who is like you, delivering the poor from him who is too strong for him?' The whole body of the righteous sufferer glorifying God anticipates the resurrection body — the body of Christ raised in glory that declares the incomparability of the LORD. Death, stronger than David's enemies, cannot hold the one whose bones speak praise.
11: direct Malicious witnesses rise up; they ask me of things I do not know. Matthew 26:59-60 describes the Sanhedrin trial precisely: the chief priests and the whole council were seeking false testimony against Jesus... many false witnesses came forward. The Passion narrative fulfills this verse structurally — malicious witnesses arising against the innocent one.
12: shadow They repay me evil for good; my soul is bereft. Christ's betrayal is the supreme instance: Judas repays three years of discipleship and love with a kiss that hands Jesus to his enemies. The crowd repays healings, feedings, and exorcisms with the cry crucify him! — evil repaid for measureless good.
13: shadow But I, when they were sick — I wore sackcloth; I afflicted myself with fasting. Christ's compassion for his enemies is revealed at Jerusalem: When he drew near and saw the city, he wept over it (Luke 19:41-44). He mourned over those who would reject him — wearing, as it were, the sackcloth of grief for those who plotted his death.
14: shadow I went about as though I grieved for my friend or my brother; I bowed down in mourning, as one who laments his mother. Christ's grief at Lazarus's tomb (John 11:35 — Jesus wept) is the incarnation of this sorrow. He mourns with those who mourn (Romans 12:15), entering fully into human grief even while being the one who overcomes death.
15: shadow But at my stumbling they rejoiced and gathered; they gathered against me. The gathering around the cross to mock is the precise fulfillment: those who passed by derided him... so also the chief priests mocked him (Matthew 27:39-41). The malicious joy of the crowd at the righteous one's stumbling is the atmosphere of the crucifixion.
16: shadow Like profane mockers at a feast, they gnash at me with their teeth. Gnashing teeth at the cross: the rage against Christ as he hangs helpless. The image appears in the Wisdom tradition for frenzied, animal-like hatred. Those who gnash teeth at the crucified Christ will ultimately gnash them in a different context — their own judgment (Matthew 8:12).
17: shadow How long, O Lord, will you look on? Rescue me from their destruction, my precious life from the lions! The how long of desolation echoes Psalms 22:1 (My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?) — the Passion cry. Christ's experience of divine absence on the cross is the supreme instance of this lament's depth, and the resurrection is God's answer to how long.
18: direct I will give you thanks in the great congregation; in the mighty throng I will praise you. Hebrews 2:12 quotes Psalms 22:22 but the structure here anticipates the same movement — the vindicated sufferer leading praise among the congregation. Christ leads his brothers in praise within the assembly (Hebrews 2:12), the great congregation of the redeemed.
19: direct Let not those rejoice over me who are wrongfully my foes, and let not those wink the eye who hate me without cause. John 15:25 cites they hated me without cause — the exact phrase shared by Psalms 35:19 and Psalms 69:4. Jesus applies it directly to himself: But the word that is written in their Law must be fulfilled: 'They hated me without a cause.'
20: shadow For they do not speak peace, but against those who are quiet in the land they devise words of deceit. The plotting of the Sanhedrin against the peaceful Christ: the chief priests and the scribes were seeking how to put him to death (Luke 22:2). Against the Prince of Peace they devise deception — Judas's betrayal is the culmination of words of deceit.
21: shadow They open wide their mouths against me; they say, 'Aha, Aha! Our eyes have seen it!' The verbal assault at the trial and cross: the high priest tearing his robes and declaring blasphemy (Matthew 26:65), the crowd's taunts (aha, you who would destroy the temple!, Mark 15:29-30). Mouths opened wide against the Servant of the LORD (Isaiah 53:7 — he opened not his mouth, in contrast).
22: shadow You have seen, O LORD; be not silent! O Lord, be not far from me! The silence of God at the cross — My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? — breaks at the resurrection. God who was not silent raised Jesus from the dead (Acts 2:24). The prayer be not far from me is answered by the Spirit's indwelling of the risen body of believers.
23: shadow Awake and rouse yourself for my vindication, for my cause, my God and my Lord! The call to God to awake anticipates resurrection language: Awake, O sleeper, and arise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you (Ephesians 5:14). The Father's rousing of himself for the Son's vindication is the resurrection — God declared Jesus to be his Son in power (Romans 1:4).
24: shadow Vindicate me, O LORD my God, according to your righteousness, and let them not rejoice over me! God vindicates the Son's righteousness through resurrection: Christ was declared to be the Son of God in power according to the Spirit of holiness by his resurrection from the dead (Romans 1:4). The Father's verdict of righteousness silences those who rejoiced at the cross.
25: shadow Let them not say in their hearts, 'Aha, our heart's desire!' Let them not say, 'We have swallowed him up.' The gloating that Christ is swallowed up by death is overturned by resurrection. Death swallowed him — but he swallows up death in victory (1 Corinthians 15:54). The enemy's claim to have consumed the righteous one is permanently reversed at the empty tomb.
26: shadow Let them be put to shame and confused altogether who rejoice at my calamity! The eschatological reversal: at the Name of Jesus every knee will bow (Philippians 2:10-11) — those who rejoiced at his calamity will confess his Lordship. The shame they intended for the cross rebounds on themselves at the parousia.
27: shadow Let those who delight in my righteousness shout for joy and be glad and say evermore, 'Great is the LORD, who delights in the welfare of his servant!' The shout of joy over Christ's righteousness is the church's cry: those clothed in his imputed righteousness (Philippians 3:9) shout evermore as they behold the welfare of the vindicated Servant of the LORD.
28: shadow Then my tongue will tell of your righteousness and of your praise all the day long. The risen Christ's proclamation parallels Hebrews 2:12 (from Psalms 22:22): the vindicated sufferer tells of God's righteousness to his brothers. The day-long praise is realized in the church's liturgy — Christ himself leads the praise of his Father through the gathered assembly.
